Except for fugitives, armies and professional explorers, discovering France with or without a map,
was largely a matter of negotiating the network of roads, rivers, canals, and railways.
From the distant present, this looks like a tale of steady progress. The speed of travel increased,
people in merchandise moved about the country with growing ease, and social and economic change arrived by carriage and locomotive instead of on the back of a peddle or a mule.
In the century that followed the revolution, the National Road Network almost doubled in size,
and the canal network increased fivefold. The 14 miles of railway in 1828 and 22,000 in 1888.
By the mid-19th century, a high speed goods vehicle could cover 50 miles of day.
Roads improved more quickly than at any time since the conquest of Gaul,
tracks that had wandered across country like a peasant returning from a feast were straightened,
steep hills were flattened by hairpin bends, violent rivers were tamed or bypassed by imperturbable canals.
One day, people would have to travel hundreds of miles to have the thrill of crossing a rickety plank bridge
or seeing a carriage wheel skittering on the brink of a precipice.
When the poet Alfred Divini watched the plume of a steam engine crossing a landscape at more than 10 miles an hour,

metropolitan France, whose express robes and canals were universally admired as engineering marbles,
but not in the other France, which was still recovering from the fall of the Roman Empire.
